metadata.summary: Decompose project scope into deliverable-oriented, hierarchically numbered work packages that are estimable, assignable, and measurable — grounded in the 100% rule and WBS dictionary.

content.title: Work Breakdown Structure Creation
content.summary: Without a WBS, estimates are guesses and dependencies are invisible until they block progress. Deliverable-oriented decomposition forces the team to answer "what will be produced?" before "how will we produce it?", which surfaces scope gaps and prevents activity-focused planning that omits unassigned outputs. The WBS dictionary transforms numbered IDs into testable acceptance criteria.

why.p: Without a WBS, estimates are guesses and dependencies are invisible until they block progress. Deliverable-oriented decomposition forces the team to answer "what will be produced?" before "how will we produce it?", which surfaces scope gaps and prevents activity-focused planning that omits unassigned outputs. The WBS dictionary transforms numbered IDs into testable acceptance criteria.

list.item: New projects where scope is fixed enough to decompose into deliverables (more than four weeks of work).
Fixed-bid proposals where each work package needs an hours/cost line for the estimate.
Programs requiring a contractual scope baseline with WBS IDs for traceability.
Migration or cutover projects where a missing deliverable is expensive.
Pure-Scrum backlog work where the product backlog serves the same role.
Exploratory R&D — the deliverables are unknown before the work begins.
Single-week tasks — a checklist is faster.
Continuous operations or service catalog work — use the service catalog instead.

rule.statement: Every WBS node name must be a noun (deliverable or sub-deliverable), never a verb or activity; verbs belong in the schedule, not the WBS.
rule.rationale: Activity-oriented WBSs silently drift into Gantt charts, losing the deliverable focus that enables acceptance criteria, traceability, and cost rollup. A noun-named WBS forces the team to answer "what will exist?" before "what will we do?"

example.title: Verb-led WBS names
example.note: Design database schema, Implement user authentication, Test payment integration — these are schedule activities, not deliverables.

example.title: Noun-led WBS names
example.note: Database schema, User authentication module, Payment integration — completion of each produces a testable artifact.

section.name: hierarchical-decomposition
section.p: WBS levels follow a standard hierarchy:

list.item: Level 1 — Project (one node, the project name).
Level 2 — Major deliverables (product components, project phases, or functional areas).
Level 3 — Sub-deliverables within each major deliverable.
Level 4 — Work packages: the lowest level, estimable and assignable.

rule.statement: Limit WBS depth to five levels maximum; branches deeper than five are over-decomposed and belong in the task list or schedule, not the WBS.
rule.rationale: Over-decomposition produces a WBS that functions as a task list, losing its value as a high-level scope map. Level 5 work packages are typically under eight hours and cannot be meaningfully baselined or controlled at the WBS level.

rule.statement: At every level, the sum of child nodes must capture 100% of the parent's scope — no more, no less; verify by asking "if all children are complete, is the parent complete?"
rule.rationale: 100% rule violations are the primary cause of forgotten scope; missing items at any level create work that must be absorbed informally, driving schedule and cost overrun without appearing as a scope change.

rule.statement: Always include a "Project Management" branch (e.g., 1.X) capturing planning, status reporting, stakeholder management, and project closure; projects that omit PM scope underestimate effort by 10-20%.
rule.rationale: PM work is real work; not scoping it creates a structural underestimate that forces the PM to absorb costs from other work packages, distorting EVM indices and schedule performance.

section.name: work-package-criteria
section.p: A valid work package meets all four criteria:

list.item: Estimable — can be assigned a credible hours or days estimate (8-80 hour guideline).
Assignable — can be delegated to one person or one team with clear accountability.
Measurable — has a clear "done" criterion against which completion can be verified.
Mutually exclusive — does not overlap with any sibling work package.

section.name: wbs-dictionary

rule.statement: Write a WBS dictionary entry for every work package before locking the baseline; numbers without definitions confuse estimators and make acceptance criteria disputes inevitable.
rule.rationale: WBS IDs become references in contracts, schedules, RTMs, and risk registers; without a dictionary, WBS-1.2.3 means different things to different readers, breaking traceability at every integration point.

section.p: Required dictionary fields per work package: WBS ID, name, description, deliverable (what is produced), acceptance criteria (how completion is verified), owner, predecessor WBS IDs, effort estimate, cost estimate.

rule.statement: Never renumber WBS IDs after baseline lock; mark removed nodes as "deprecated" and their ID as retired; IDs are referenced by contracts, schedules, and registers that cannot be retroactively updated.
rule.rationale: Re-numbering after baseline creates a version mismatch: change requests reference WBS-1.2.3 which no longer exists in the current structure, making the audit trail unresolvable.

rule.statement: Lock the WBS at the work-package level for the baseline; detail below work-package level (individual tasks, sub-tasks) belongs in the schedule and can evolve without a scope change request.
rule.rationale: Locking task-level detail in the WBS baseline makes minor scheduling adjustments into scope changes, which adds bureaucratic overhead without improving control.

section.name: agentic-wbs-authoring

rule.statement: The WBS-authoring agent must produce noun-led names only; the system prompt must enforce this constraint because LLMs default to activity-oriented (verb-led) decomposition.
rule.rationale: LLMs are trained on project management content that mixes WBS and task-list formats; without explicit enforcement, the output looks like a schedule, not a WBS, and the deliverable focus is lost.

rule.statement: The agent must emit a coverage rationale per parent node ("this node is 100% covered by its children because...") to support the 100% rule verification that human reviewers cannot mechanically verify at scale.
rule.rationale: Automated 100% rule checking is difficult without domain knowledge; the agent's coverage rationale shifts the verification burden to explaining its reasoning, which human reviewers can evaluate faster than checking completeness from scratch.

section.note: Recommended subagents: faion-pm-agent (owns WBS authoring; drafts hierarchy from scope statement), faion-sdd-planning agent (connects WBS work packages to SDD task breakdown), faion-business-analyst agent (useful for requirements-to-WBS traceability matrix). LLMs love to write activity-oriented WBSs; system prompt must enforce noun-led naming. Agents over-decompose ("Login button color" as a leaf) — cap depth at 5 in the prompt. 100% rule is hard to verify automatically; require the agent to emit a coverage rationale per parent. Never let the agent renumber the WBS after baseline — IDs are referenced by contracts, schedules, and registers. WBS dictionaries written by LLMs read generic; seed each entry with the parent's acceptance criteria text, not just the name. Human-in-loop checkpoints: (1) initial draft review, (2) 100%-rule validation, (3) baseline lock with sponsor sign-off.

section.name: limitations-and-anti-patterns
section.p: Activity-oriented WBSs (verbs everywhere) silently drift toward Gantt charts and lose deliverable focus. Inconsistent depth across branches — one branch detailed to level 5, another stays at level 2 — produces inconsistent work-package sizes. 100% rule violations are invisible without explicit verification. WBS Dictionary is the part everyone skips, then estimates become hand-waving. For multi-team programs, naming collisions (two "API" branches) break traceability.

antipattern.description: Inconsistent decomposition depth across branches — one branch detailed to level 5, another stays at level 2.
antipattern.reason: Inconsistent depth produces inconsistent work-package sizes; large packages are unestimable and hide dependencies, small packages add tracking overhead without control benefit. Aim for consistent 40-80 hour work packages across the WBS.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Use nouns at every level; activities belong in the schedule, not the WBS. Always include a "1.0 Project Management" branch — captures planning, status, closure work. Number every node (1.2.3 style) and never reuse numbers when refactoring; mark removed nodes as deprecated. Lock the WBS at the work-package level for the baseline; deeper detail can evolve via the schedule. Cross-reference each work package to (a) requirements, (b) acceptance criteria, (c) responsible owner. For SDD repos, mirror leaf work packages as files in .aidocs/ so the structure is grep-able.

template.purpose: WBS outline template in Markdown outline format
WBS dictionary entry template for a single work package

script.purpose: Lints a YAML WBS for verb-led names, depth violations, and missing PM branch

commands.command: Lint WBS for naming discipline, depth violations, and structure issues

see-also.link: PMI PMBOK Guide 7th ed., Planning Performance Domain
PMI Practice Standard for Work Breakdown Structures, 3rd ed.
G. Haugan, Effective Work Breakdown Structures (2002)
DoD MIL-STD-881F — WBS for defense programs
